JOURNAL (current thesis state):
# Journal — Investing Agent

## 1. Market view

Universe mixed-to-soft with unusual insider activity this week — Arnhult's Corem conviction buy opened the Aggressive sleeve for the first time. Cash at 70.8%, six positions across five sectors. Holding pattern into the July earnings cluster (Volvo, Essity, Thule all reporting Jul 23–29; ATRLJ ~Jul 10; CORE-B ~Jul 22). No urgency to deploy further before those prints resolve. Vimian and ASSA remain live but patient.

## 2. Holdings

**HM-B.ST** — Core | Consumer Discretionary | 5.0%
- **Thesis:** Karl-Johan Persson bought ~1.2 BSEK across six transactions in eight days into an 11% drawdown — among the strongest insider signals possible on Swedish Large Cap. Starter position pending fundamental confirmation.
- **Sell if:** Profit warning, gross margin collapse, or confirmed structural market-share loss to Shein/Temu. Not on price weakness alone.
- **Status:** `intact`

**ESSITY-B.ST** — Core | Consumer Staples | 8.0%
- **Thesis:** Exceptional 90-day insider cluster — 512 MSEK, 7 buyers, 0 sellers — led by Industrivärden. Defensive staples compounder at ~14x P/E with 3.4% yield. Q2 print ~Jul 24 is the next adjudicator.
- **Sell if:** Sustained margin deterioration where input costs structurally exceed pricing power, or Q2 shows revenue decline accelerating with margin compression. Not on price weakness alone.
- **Status:** `intact`

**THULE.ST** — Core | Consumer Discretionary | 3.6%
- **Thesis:** Ten insiders including chair, vice chair, CEO, and CFO bought ~14–25 MSEK across two waves, zero sellers. Quality mid-cap franchise 28% off highs; weakness is cyclical not structural. Vice chairman's ~11.6 MSEK personal buy in mid-May confirms conviction post-drawdown.
- **Sell if:** Insider turns net seller, operating margin compresses below ~13%, or Q2 (~Jul 29) reveals genuine earnings deterioration rather than cyclical softness.
- **Status:** `intact`

**VOLV-B.ST** — Core | Industrials | 4.7%
- **Thesis:** Industrivärden deployed ~290 MSEK in a single day near 52-week highs. Net cash ~58 BSEK, ~4% yield, 9.8% operating margin despite -9.5% YoY revenue. Controlling owner buying near highs is a strong forward-earnings endorsement. Q2 ~Jul 24.
- **Sell if:** Industrivärden becomes net seller, profit warning, or Q2 reveals order intake collapse rather than cyclical normalisation. Revenue decline alone is not a sell trigger if margins hold.
- **Status:** `intact`

**ATRLJ-B.ST** — Core | Real Estate | 4.9%
- **Thesis:** Tagehus (controlling family) bought ~30 MSEK into Varma's forced 7%-discount block exit. Ericsson 58k sqm Hagastaden anchor lease provides fundamental confirmation. Starter size only given net debt/equity 101%.
- **Sell if:** Further forced-seller overhang materialises without insider response; Q2 (~Jul 10) reveals deteriorating occupancy/lease renewals; or Ericsson lease proves less economically material than signalled.
- **Status:** `intact`

**CORE-B.ST** — Aggressive | Real Estate | 3.0%
- **Thesis:** Rutger Arnhult (controlling owner/CEO) bought ~110 MSEK personally across two waves into a 50%-off, capitulating leveraged commercial real estate name — clearest Aggressive sleeve signal seen this cycle. FCF positive (678 MSEK R12); this is a leverage/rate-sensitivity story, not a going-concern story. Starter size only given net debt/equity 185%.
- **Sell if:** Dilutive equity raise announced, covenant breach disclosed, or Q2 (~Jul 22) reveals LTV deterioration or occupancy collapse. Arnhult becoming a net seller would be immediate exit.
- **Status:** `intact`

## 3. Watchlist

**VIMIAN.ST** — ~49 MSEK insider cluster (Fidelio Vet + CEO, Jun 12) on a quality animal-health compounder; +17.7% 3m recovery underway. Held back by P/E ~40 and negative FCF. Revisit after Q2 print ~Jul 29 before adding a second Aggressive position.

**ASSA-B.ST** — CEO bought ~35 MSEK across April and June on a quality compounder; stock at 332 vs 315 entry target. Q2 ~Jul 28. Watch for price reaching 315 SEK or post-print entry.

**NOLA-B.ST** — Quality industrial (10.8% op margin, clean balance sheet) at 23% drawdown with CEO transition overhang and shrinking revenue. Screener's "10.8 MSEK insider buy" was misleading — actual disclosed buy only 205k SEK. Revisit after Q2 ~Aug 5 and new CEO's first framing.

**LIFCO-B.ST** — April insider cluster (3 buyers, ~12 MSEK) still valid but stock rallied +10.5% 1m; original 270 SEK anchor stale. Revised entry zone 275–285 SEK. Revisit post-Q2 ~Jul 24 if price resets.

**SCST.ST** — Exceptional 8-buyer / 30 MSEK cluster including strategic peer Grupo Lusiaves. Still extended (+25% above 200MA). Revisit if price pulls back to ~135 SEK or post-Jul 28 earnings.

## 4. Lessons learned / open questions

- **Aggressive sleeve discipline:** CORE-B is the first use of the Aggressive sleeve — correctly sized small (~3%), entered on the clearest possible signal (controlling owner, personal capital, two waves). The sleeve exists for exactly this; the multi-week hesitation to open it was correct patience, not timidity.
- **Correlated real estate exposure:** Both ATRLJ and CORE-B are leveraged Swedish commercial real estate — partially correlated. Combined ~7.9%. Sector cap is 25% but concentration risk is real; no further real estate adds without rebalancing one of these.
- **Screener signal vs disclosed filing:** NOLA-B screener angle ("10.8 MSEK insider buy") did not match FI disclosure (205k SEK). Reinforces standing rule: cross-check every cluster signal against actual filings before acting. This is the same screener hygiene issue flagged last week.
- **Open question:** Four positions report in a 12-day window (ATRLJ ~Jul 10, CORE-B ~Jul 22, Volvo/Essity ~Jul 24, Thule ~Jul 29). Consider whether any pre-print trimming is warranted, or whether current sizes (all ≤8%) provide sufficient cushion across what is effectively one concentrated reporting season.

New-name insider signals (not currently held — you may open these):
  - TEL2-B.ST: significant insider buying in last 2 days — see Analyst note below
  - TEL2-A.ST: significant insider buying in last 2 days — see Analyst note below
  - RUSTA.ST: significant insider buying in last 2 days — see Analyst note below
Material news today (>=M3):
  - THULE.ST: M3 Thule CEO Daniel Larsson sells shares worth ~1 million SEK.

ANALYST NOTES on new (not-yet-held) names surfaced today:
# Tele2 B (TEL2-B.ST) — Analyst Note

**Thesis.** Tele2 is a mature Swedish telco throwing off serious cash (FCF yield ~10%, div yield ~6%) at a reasonable P/E of 12. The interesting event this week is a very large block sale by Freya Investissement (~5.4 bn SEK on 2026-06-15), which has pressured the price down ~5% in a month. If that's a one-off ownership reshuffle rather than a thesis break, the dip is buyable into a high-quality dividend payer.

**Positives**
- Strong cash generation: FCF 12.5 bn SEK on 30 bn revenue, FCF yield ~10% — unusually high for a defensive telco.
- New CEO bought ~2.3 MSEK personally on 28–29 May, a credibility signal at the top of the house just before the Freya sell-down.
- Dividend yield 5.94% with P/E only 12.1 — pays you to wait, and ROE 35% suggests the payout is well covered.
- Operating margin 40.4% and net margin 33.7% indicate genuine pricing power / cost discipline, not just accounting.

**Concerns**
- The 5.4 bn SEK Freya block sale is the dominant data point — without knowing whether this was strategic exit, forced liquidity, or partial trim, I can't fully read the signal. I cannot evaluate this from the data I have.
- Net debt 24.7 bn SEK at 86% of equity is meaningful for a telco; rising rates or capex shocks would bite.
- Telco revenue growth is structurally low (1.4% YoY, 3y CAGR 2.1%) — this is a yield/cash-return story, not growth.
- EFN flagged Kinnevik missing out on a dividend — if there's broader pressure on the payout from major holders, that undermines half the thesis. Worth watching.
- Earnings in ~36 days; large insider/holder churn right before a print is something to respect.

**Insider read.** Mixed but tilted constructive at the operator level. New CEO and another manager bought small-but-real personal stakes in late May; CFO and a few managers sold modest amounts in April. The 5.4 bn Freya sale dwarfs all of it in SEK terms but is an institutional ownership event, not an operating-insider signal — I'd weight the small CEO/management buys more for thesis purposes.

**Price read.** Px 176.85, down 5.4% in a month and 7.4% in 3m, but still +25% over 12m and +4.4% above the 200-day MA. The pullback brings it back near trend rather than into clear value territory; -10.5% from 52w high is a shallow drawdown.

**Verdict.** `WATCH — not actionable now, keep on watchlist`

The combination of high FCF, fresh CEO insider buy, and a pullback is attractive, but the Freya block sale is too big and too recent to ignore without context, and earnings are 5 weeks away. I'd want to see the Q2 print (dividend posture, debt trajectory) before committing capital. A clean buy zone would be closer to 165 if the pullback continues without a thesis break.

# Tele2 A (TEL2-A.ST) — Analyst Note

**Thesis.** Tele2 is a mature Swedish telecom throwing off substantial free cash flow (FCF yield ~10%) and paying a ~6% dividend at a reasonable P/E of 12. The recent ~8% three-month pullback into the 200-day MA creates a plausible entry into a defensive cash-return story — but there is no fresh catalyst here, and the insider tape, while noisy, leans mildly negative on management selling.

**Positives**
- Strong cash generation: FCF 12.5 BSEK on revenue ~30 BSEK, supporting the 5.97% dividend yield comfortably.
- Reported margins are exceptional (operating 40.4%, net 33.7%) and ROE 35% suggests efficient capital use — though I'd want to verify these aren't flattered by one-offs (see concerns).
- Valuation undemanding: P/E 12, ~10% FCF yield — defensive telecom characteristics at a non-stretched multiple.
- Pullback to near 200-day MA (only +3.7% above) after a strong 12m (+24.8%) offers a less extended entry point.

**Concerns**
- Net debt 24.7 BSEK (85.7% of equity) is meaningful for a low-growth business; dividend sustainability depends on continued FCF discipline.
- Revenue growth is anemic (+1.4% YoY, 3y CAGR 2.1%) — this is a yield story, not a growth story. Any FCF disappointment would hit hard.
- Three management insiders sold in late April (CFO included, ~7.6 MSEK combined) — small in absolute terms but the cluster post-Q1 is mildly negative signal. The huge Freya Investissement buy/sell pair appears to be a derivatives hedge unwind, not directional.
- Operating margin of 40%+ on a telecom looks unusually high — I cannot verify from the data I have whether this reflects a one-off gain, asset sale, or accounting treatment. Worth flagging before committing capital.
- No catalyst in sight; next earnings ~36 days away.

**Insider read.** Mildly negative. The Freya transaction nets to zero and looks structural. The three management sells (including CFO Peter Landgren) clustered around the Q1 print are small but not encouraging — no offsetting management buying.

**Price read.** Price at 176, down 6.4% in the last month and 12% off the 52w high, sitting just above the 200-day MA. Technically a reasonable level, not a screaming entry.

**Verdict:** WATCH — not actionable now, keep on watchlist

The yield + valuation combination is genuinely attractive, but with (a) insider selling rather than buying post-Q1, (b) suspiciously high reported margins I cannot verify, (c) Q2 print only ~5 weeks away, and (d) no specific trigger for action today, the right move is to wait for the Q2 report to confirm FCF and margin sustainability. If post-Q2 the story holds and price is in the 165–175 range, this becomes a Core candidate.

```json
}
```

# Rusta (RUSTA.ST) — Analyst Note

**Thesis.** Discount retailer down ~25% in a month despite reporting a profit increase on June 9, and a major shareholder (Aforber Invest) immediately responded with ~52 MSEK of open-market buying on June 11–12. Fundamentals are solid (mid-single-digit growth, 10% FCF yield, 25% ROE) and the price weakness looks like a sentiment dislocation rather than a thesis break — though I cannot verify that without reading the actual Q4 report.

**Positives**
- R12 revenue +6.5% YoY with a 3-year CAGR of 7.3% — consistent growth, not a one-off.
- FCF of 1,183 MSEK on a market cap implying ~10% FCF yield is genuinely cheap for a growing retailer.
- Insider signal is unusually clean: one named buyer (Aforber Invest AB), ~52 MSEK across 12+ transactions in two days, zero sellers — this is a conviction buy on the dip, not noise.
- The June 9 print was a "profit increase" per gp.se, yet the stock fell hard afterwards — suggests the sell-off is about guidance/sentiment, not realized numbers.

**Concerns**
- Net debt / equity of 243% is high. For a discount retailer this is likely lease-driven (IFRS 16), but I can't confirm from this data — if it's real financial debt, the FCF yield matters less.
- I don't have the actual Q4 report, so I cannot tell *why* the stock dropped 25% in a month. There is almost certainly bad news in there (margin guidance? consumer outlook? specific Q4 weakness behind the R12 headline?) that the market is reacting to and I cannot see.
- 30-day realized vol of 50% confirms this is in a stress regime — knife-catching risk is real.
- Operating margin 7.6% / net 4.4% is thin; a consumer slowdown bites quickly at this margin profile.
- Next earnings ~296 days away — long wait for a fresh data point to confirm or break the thesis.

**Insider read.** Strong positive. Aforber Invest is a known holder (the name suggests a family/principal vehicle) buying ~52 MSEK in the open market within days of the report, with no offsetting sells. On a Swedish mid-cap, a single buyer of this size on a post-print dip is one of the higher-quality signals available.

**Price read.** Down 25% in a month, 16% in three months, but still +5% over 12 months and only 5.5% below the 200-day MA — so this is a sharp correction off the highs (27% below 52-week high), not a multi-year downtrend. Aforber's buying clustered around current levels (~75 SEK), which is a useful anchor.

**Verdict.** This is the classic setup the agent is built to notice: post-report dislocation + clean insider conviction buy + reasonable valuation. But I can't see the report itself, and the leverage figure needs explanation. Sized small (Aggressive sleeve, half-position) it's actionable; sized as a Core holding it requires reading the Q4 report first.

**Verdict: INTERESTING — fits Aggressive sleeve**

Suggest Aggressive sleeve, half-position to start, given I can't verify the report contents. Thesis breaks if: another insider sells, Aforber stops buying near these levels, or any follow-up news clarifies a real operational problem behind the price drop."

RISK CAPS (mirrors constitution §4 — enforced in code):
  - Max 15% single holding (of total portfolio)
  - Max 25% any single sector (of total)
  - Max 10% single Aggressive position
  - Max 20% Aggressive sleeve total
  - Min 30% cash buffer of the Core sleeve
  - Max 90% total equity exposure
  - Max ~10 holdings
  - Min 4-week holding period — early full exit requires thesis_break=True + rationale
response_text:
**Trigger read.** Three things on the tape: (1) Thule CEO sold ~981k SEK — first sell after the buy cluster, small, single transaction, does *not* meet the "net seller" sell trigger; (2) two TEL2 lines — Analyst says WATCH on both, unresolved Freya block and Q2 five weeks out; (3) RUSTA — Analyst flags this as INTERESTING/Aggressive, time-sensitive Aforber cluster from Jun 11–12.

**Decision.**

*THULE:* Do nothing. One CEO sell of ~1 MSEK after he and nine others bought ~14–25 MSEK does not break the thesis or meet the explicit "net seller" trigger. Note it, watch for follow-on sells into Q2 (Jul 29). Trimming on this would be exactly the price-driven, news-light reaction the constitution warns against.

*TEL2 (both lines):* No action — Analyst verdict is WATCH on both, conviction 2, Q2 print is the gate. The Freya 5.4 bn block is exactly the kind of unresolved ownership event we don't front-run. Sunday-or-later decision.

*RUSTA:* This is the one. Setup matches CORE-B pattern: single named buyer (Aforber Invest), ~52 MSEK clustered Jun 11–12, into a 25% post-print dislocation on a *profit-increase* report, FCF yield ~10%, growing top-line. Signal is fresh (4 days old) and would degrade by Sunday — Aforber's buying may complete and the dislocation may close. Constitution test: does this genuinely need to act today rather than Sunday? Yes — a fresh single-buyer cluster on a post-print dip is precisely a time-sensitive opportunity.

Sizing: Aggressive sleeve, half-position per Analyst — I can't read the Q4 report myself, and the 243% net debt/equity (likely IFRS 16 leases, but unverified) argues for caution. Take 3% of portfolio (~3,000 SEK), in line with CORE-B's starter size. That keeps the Aggressive sleeve at ~6% — well inside the 20% cap — and leaves room to add post-Q4-verification.

At ~75 SEK reference: 40 shares ≈ 3,000 SEK.

**Risk awareness.** After this trade: cash ~67.8k (68%), Aggressive sleeve ~6% (cap 20%), no single-name or sector breach. Real Estate stays untouched. All good.

```json
    {

    }
  ],

}
```
